data.title: Allspring Common Stock Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Strategy

Public equity markets are often driven by emotion, requiring successful investors to have conviction in individual securities and diversification across sectors.

Our team's conviction comes from an in-depth private market valuation (PMV, the price an acquirer would pay to purchase the entire company) process of analyzing the business model, competitive positioning, key trends, management, and other proprietary metrics.

We believe that the PMV of a company is much more stable than its associated public market stock price.

Our proprietary PMV investment process provides us with a consistent and accurate discipline to exploit market emotion by buying company stock at a discount to their PMV and selling them as they get fully valued.

Quarterly review

  • The Allspring Common Stock Fund outperformed the Russell 2500 Index during the period.
  • Stock selection in industrials and consumer discretionary sectors contributed to relative portfolio performance while selection in information technology (IT) impeded quarterly results.

Market review

Markets closed the year on a strong note, with momentum building in the final months despite ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty. The fourth quarter brought a broad rebound as inflation eased and the Federal Reserve (Fed) shifted to a more supportive stance, cutting rates by 75 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) across three meetings. Investor confidence improved as trade tensions cooled, and leadership rotated toward industrials, semiconductors, and select health care names while consumer staples and discretionary sectors lagged. Artificial intelligence (AI)-related companies remained in focus, though concerns about profitability and leverage tempered enthusiasm late in the quarter. Against this backdrop, small- and mid-cap stocks posted modest gains, with the Russell 2500 Index up 2.22% for the quarter, and active strategies benefited from targeted exposure to industrials and health care.

For the year, equity markets delivered their third consecutive annual advance. The S&P 500 Index rose 18%, the Nasdaq was up 20%, and the Dow Jones Industrial Average added nearly 13%. Gains were concentrated in large-cap technology names, leaving the equal-weight S&P 500 Index trailing by more than 700 bps. AI optimism dominated headlines, while sectors such as managed care, apparel, and commodity chemicals performed poorly. Early-year volatility, when the S&P 500 Index fell nearly 19% amid tariff uncertainty, gave way to recovery as trade concerns eased and monetary policy turned accommodative. Despite these tailwinds, active managers faced challenges, with only 29.7% outperforming the S&P 500 Index, underscoring the difficulty of navigating concentrated leadership and the need for disciplined research. In addition, throughout 2025, the equity market was characterized by a persistent “momentum trade” that often decoupled from fundamental valuations, as the top-performing decile increasingly included companies with high price velocity but negative or inconsistent profitability. This speculative fervor around emerging technologies allowed non-earning companies to outperform high-quality, cash-flow-positive peers, driving market concentration to historic levels and heightening the risk of a sharp technical reversal.

Fund performance and attribution

The Allspring Common Stock Fund outperformed the Russell 2500 Index during the period. Gains were supported by favorable stock selection and sector allocation, with industrials as a key contributor while consumer discretionary also added incremental strength. IT remained a headwind, reflecting ongoing volatility in tech-related areas amid heightened scrutiny of AI-driven business models.

The broader market backdrop was mixed, with resilient consumer spending and strength in select cyclical sectors offset by softness in labor markets and uneven performance across technology segments. Against this environment, the portfolio’s positioning in areas tied to industrial demand and select consumer names provided a measure of stability and relative advantage.

For the full year, the strategy faced a challenging landscape and ultimately lagged its benchmark, as persistent weakness in IT weighed heavily on results. While sector allocation offered a modest offset, negative stock selection across several segments proved difficult to overcome. The year was marked by significant macroeconomic uncertainty, including tariff-driven trade disruptions and shifting sentiment around AI that contributed to pronounced swings in market leadership. Despite these headwinds, the portfolio maintained a disciplined approach, emphasizing diversification and long-term positioning, even as market narratives evolved rapidly throughout the year.

Top Contributors And Detractors To Quarter-End Fund Performance

Contributors

  • Revolve Group, Inc. Class A (RVLV)
  • Haemonetics Corp. (HAE)
  • Kirby Corp. (KEX)
  • Bruker Corp. (BRKR)
  • Allient Inc. (ALNT)

Detractors

  • Chewy, Inc. Class A (CHWY)
  • Teledyne Technologies Inc. (TDY)
  • AAON, Inc. (AAON)
  • National Vision Holdings, Inc. (EYE)
  • CCC Intelligent Solutions Holdings Inc. (CCC)

The holdings identified do not represent all of the securities purchased or sold during the period shown and should not be construed as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. Information on calculation methodology and a list showing the overall contribution of each holding in the account for the period shown are available upon request.

Stocks Contributing To Relative Performance Include:

Revolve Group, Inc. (RVLV), operates an online fashion platform offering apparel and accessories primarily to millennial and Gen Z consumers, generating revenue through direct-to-consumer sales. In the fourth quarter, Revolve’s stock rose as its revenue met expectations with a 4.4% year-over-year increase. Gross margins outperformed, surpassing forecasts by 100 bps compared with last year and 150 bps above market estimates, due to tighter promotions and effective inventory management. Despite concerns about potential margin pressures from tariffs, these fears have not materialized, and RVLV has continued to gain market share.

Haemonetics Corp. (HAE) provides blood management solutions and related technologies to health care providers, generating revenue through systems and services that support plasma collection, hospital procedures, and blood center operations. In fiscal second quarter 2026, Haemonetics delivered strong performance, raising guidance and posting margin gains that signal operational discipline. The company’s differentiated portfolio—spanning plasma technologies and hospital solutions—positions it well to capture share in high-growth segments. Haemonetics outperformed the benchmark this quarter, supported by broad-based execution and favorable mix shifts.

Recent catalysts include momentum in plasma and hospital businesses alongside benefits from portfolio realignment. Momentum in the plasma segment could be fueled by a stretched low-income consumer. The hospital segment has gone through a leadership transition that reset numbers lower. Our belief is they are now positioned to grow at a high-single-digit percentage as we look out 12 months.

Bruker Corp. (BRKR) develops and supplies scientific instruments and analytical solutions for research and industrial applications, generating revenue through product sales and related services. BRKR delivered strong performance in the quarter, outpacing peers with a robust earnings beat, and raised forward guidance that bolstered investor confidence. Growth was driven by strong demand for scientific instruments and life science tools as research activity stabilized. Strategic investments in innovation and new markets, combined with operating leverage, favorable product mix, and effective cost control, supported better-than-expected margins. Easing concerns around China exposure and macroeconomic uncertainty, along with improved sentiment toward life science tools, contributed to multiple expansion and relative stock outperformance versus the benchmark.

Stocks Detracting From Relative Performance Include:

Chewy, Inc., Class A (CHWY) operates an e-commerce platform focused on pet products and services, generating revenue through direct-to-consumer sales and subscription programs. CHWY shares declined in September after the company reported elevated selling, general, and administrative expenses. Even though third quarter results released in December were positive—with customer growth rebounding (+5% as net ads are improving sequentially), total revenue growth of +8.3% (implying continued market share gains), and margin expansion (gross margin up 50 bps year over year and adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization margin up 100 bps)—the stock stayed in a trading range. Notably, management highlighted expectations of stronger operating leverage in fiscal-year 2026, which we see as an important factor for the stock's outlook next year.

AAON, Inc. (AAON), is a leader in highly configurable HVAC solutions for commercial, industrial, and data center environments, emphasizing efficiency and performance. In the quarter, AAON produced strong revenue growth driven by data center customers, very strong data center orders, strong orders for traditional HVAC equipment, and improved operational performance after recent missteps. Despite that, the stock performed poorly after initial enthusiasm around its earnings report due to lingering enterprise resource planning (ERP)-driven operational concerns and worries around fluid competitive conditions in the data center HVAC market. AAON brings unique customized thermal management solutions for customers in the fast-growing data center market, as validated by extreme orders growth and backlog expansion over the past year. In the traditional rooftop business, the company is poised to see a growth inflection in 2026 as they lap cyclical pressures and operational disruptions that affected its ability to ship product in 2025. Both businesses should see material revenue growth in 2026+, which, combined with the completion of recent capacity expansions and improved ERP implementation performance, should drive a positive inflection in earnings growth.

National Vision Holdings, Inc. (EYE), operates optical retail stores and provides eye exams and eyewear products, generating revenue through a mix of managed-care partnerships and direct consumer sales. In the fourth quarter, National Vision faced a more challenging backdrop as pricing tailwinds moderated and operational complexity weighed on sentiment. The company’s scale and ability to serve value-conscious customers remain key differentiators in a competitive category. National Vision underperformed the benchmark this quarter in spite of strong fundamentals, including accelerating comparison-store sales. The stock was pressured by a 2026 outlook that reflects more surgical price-taking, which has been a key driver over the past year of the positive inflection in the business. The goodness that drove the stock through much of 2025 may be more difficult to come by in the coming year as we anniversary the fourth quarter and first quarter pricing they took.

Fund positioning and outlook

We expect markets to remain sensitive to macroeconomic developments, including the trajectory of Fed policy and ongoing trade negotiations. While consensus points to further rate cuts and easing inflation, uncertainty around tariff structures and regulatory shifts could sustain volatility. At the same time, secular growth drivers, particularly in IT and industrial innovation, continue to offer compelling opportunities for active managers. With market leadership likely to broaden beyond mega-cap names, we believe a disciplined focus on fundamentals and selective exposure to companies with strong balance sheets and pricing power should be critical to delivering long-term value.

As we enter 2026, we remain steadfast in our commitment to valuation discipline and broad-based diversification, ensuring that our portfolios are not overexposed to any one theme or factor. By focusing on durable businesses and maintaining a selective, nimble approach, we believe we are well positioned to navigate potential volatility and capture the long-term growth opportunities that emerge when the market inevitably returns to a broader focus on value.

BENCHMARK DESCRIPTIONS:

The Russell 2500™ Index measures the performance of the small- to mid-cap segment of the U.S. equity universe. It is a subset of the Russell 3000® Index and includes approximately 2,500 of the smallest securities based on a combination of their market capitalization and current index membership. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The S&P 500 Index consists of 500 stocks chosen for market size, liquidity, and industry group representation. It is a market-value-weighted index with each stock's weight in the index proportionate to its market value. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Nasdaq Composite Index measures the market value of all domestic and foreign common stocks, representing a wide array of more than 5,000 companies, listed on the Nasdaq Stock Market. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average is a price-weighted index of 30 blue-chip industrial U.S. stocks. It is considered a gauge of the broader U.S. economy. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Lipper averages are compiled by Lipper, Inc., an independent mutual fund research and rating service. Each Lipper average represents a universe of funds that are similar in investment objective. You cannot invest directly in a Lipper average.

The Morningstar Category average is the average return for the peer group based on the returns of each individual fund within the group. The total return of the Morningstar Category average does not include the effect of sales charges. You cannot invest directly in a Morningstar Category average.

Diversification does not ensure or guarantee better performance and cannot eliminate the risk of investment losses.

ATTRIBUTION ANALYSIS:

Performance attribution and sector returns are calculated using the Brinson-Fachler attribution model. As such, performance attribution calculations may differ from the fund’s actual investment results. Common sources of attribution dispersion from official returns include: the use of end of day holdings and prices, the exclusion of fees, and the use of myriad pricing sources to accommodate different benchmark providers.

DEFINITION OF TERMS:

Alpha measures the difference between a fund’s actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Downside capture measures a fund’s replication of its benchmark during periods of negative returns. During periods of negative benchmark returns, a downside capture ratio less than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and a downside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Information ratio measures the consistency of excess return (return in excess of a benchmark). This value is determined by taking the annualized excess return over a benchmark (style benchmark by default) and dividing it by the standard deviation of excess return.

R-squared is a measurement of how similar a fund’s historical performance has been to that of the benchmark. The measure ranges from 0.0, which means that the fund’s performance bears no relationship to the performance of the index, to 1.0, which means that the fund’s performance was perfectly synchronized with the performance of the benchmark.

Sharpe ratio measures the potential reward offered by a mutual fund relative to its risk level. The ratio uses a fund’s standard deviation and its excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. The higher the Sharpe ratio, the better the fund’s historical risk-adjusted performance.

Standard deviation represents the degree to which an investment’s performance has varied from its average performance over a particular time period.

Tracking error measures the extent to which a manager’s performance mimics that of a benchmark. The value is the standard deviation of the difference between a fund’s performance and a benchmark’s performance.

Upside capture measures a fund’s replication of its benchmark during periods of positive returns. During periods of positive benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Risks: Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Stock values fluctuate in response to the activities of individual companies and general market and economic conditions. Smaller company stocks tend to be more volatile and less liquid than those of larger companies. Consult the fund’s prospectus for additional information on these and other risks.

The Morningstar Rating for funds, or star rating, is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar risk-adjusted return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The Morningstar Rating does not include any adjustment for sales loads. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36–59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60–119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods.

Across U.S.-domiciled mid-cap blend funds, the Common Stock Fund received 1 star among 368 funds, 1 star among 338 funds, and 1 star among 263 funds for the 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods, respectively. The Morningstar Rating is for the Institutional Class only; other classes may have different performance characteristics.

The Morningstar absolute ranking is based on the fund’s total return rank relative to all funds that have the same category for the same time period. Morningstar rankings do not include the effect of sales charges. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

data.title: Armanino Foods May Experience Margin Pressures Soon
data.description:
With a new management team led by CEO Deanna Jurgens, the specialty Italian food manufacturer Armanino Foods (AMNF) is now planning to expand beyond high-end grocery stores by selling its pesto to fast-food restaurants. Armanino already has a sizable customer base in the restaurant industry, so management is confident in this plan even though the restaurant industry has been experiencing headwinds recently. The company is also planning to invest in more equipment so it can sustain its rapid growth.

Armanino reported great Q3 2025 results, with revenue rising 11.2% to $19.65 million and net income rising 16.3% to $4.77 million. The company’s gross profit also rose 22.9% to $99.3 million, and its gross profit margin rose 470 basis points to 49.5%. So, it appears that Armanino has strong pricing power in comparison to other CPG companies right now and is also improving its efficiency. This is a great result at a time when other CPG companies’ margins are falling.

It also looks like this company isn’t a hidden gem for value investors anymore. Its stock price has risen a lot in the past year, and now it’s trading at a similar valuation to other CPG companies. I still think that Armanino can continue growing, but I also think its margins are likely to come under pressure in 2026 for several reasons. One of them is that it might be hard for this company to maintain high margins while marketing its sauces to major fast-food chains. So, I think Armanino’s margins are likely to weaken and think it would be better to wait for a pullback before buying this stock.

Armanino Will Need to Justify Its Prices to Fast-Food Restaurants

In Armanino’s January 2026 investor presentation, the company explained that it’s offering restaurants convenience and flexibility. Quick-service restaurants won’t have to prepare their own sauces if they buy them from Armanino, so it will be easier for them to add new limited-time menu items without making their cooks do additional work. The new sauces will add exciting new tastes and help the restaurants satisfy their customers and justify their menu prices. Additionally, Armanino considers itself a premium brand in comparison to other companies that sell sauces to restaurants.

If I was pitching The Marzetti Company (MZTI) to investors, I’d make similar arguments. The Marzetti Company has recently been performing well because it’s selling sauces to value-oriented fast-food chains that are also performing well in the current economy. So, fast-food restaurants are interested in buying sauces right now. But the Marzetti Company is also larger than Armanino, so it has additional scale advantages. And The Marzetti Company has a TTM gross margin of 24%, which is less than half of Armanino’s gross margin. This tells me that Armanino is not competing on price with The Marzetti Company.

So, I’d say that Armanino does have a market opportunity in quick-serve restaurants. But it must also convince restaurants to pay higher prices for its sauces at a time when restaurants are trying to compete on price and save money anywhere they can. Armanino’s Q3 2025 results show that it was able to convince its current customers in the restaurant industry that it was worth it to pay more for high-quality sauces. Now, it’s planning to make a similar pitch to larger restaurant chains. These restaurants also have strong bargaining power and may be able to force Armanino to reduce its margins.

Armanino’s Grocery Business Can Still Grow a Lot

While Armanino’s management team seems to be focused on opportunities in the QSR sector, this company could gain additional market share in the grocery sector as well. Its investor presentation shows that its products are on the shelves at subsidiaries of Kroger (KR) and Albertsons (ACI). But Armanino’s products are only available in specific regions of the US right now. So, expanded distribution is another potential growth driver for Armanino. The company already has partnerships with national distributors.

Expanded distribution, by itself, could be a significant revenue growth driver for Armanino. This company has very high margins for a CPG company, which is why it has a TTM P/S ratio of 5.15. It’s also a small company with much lower revenue than other publicly traded CPG companies. It looks like this company was previously a specialty pesto and Italian food producer that sold products to upscale grocery stores and restaurants, not a volume producer. But now, the big grocery store chains and discount stores are also stocking its products, which means it might have a larger market in 2026.

Private-Label Competition Is a Risk for Armanino

I’d say one of the main risks for Armanino is the introduction of private-label sauces by grocery stores. One of the reasons why CPG companies have been limiting their price increases recently is because grocery stores have been stocking more private-label products. Grocery stores are trying to maintain their competitiveness as well, and many of them have been posting weaker sales growth recently.

Additionally, grocery stores aren’t just competing on price with their private-label products anymore. Health-food stores often create high-end private-label products. And major chains like Kroger and Albertsons would also be capable of developing their own premium, private-label pesto if Armanino’s products rapidly gained popularity.

Additional Risks for Armanino

This is an OTC stock that was previously trading on the pink sheets. Additionally, some of its recent financial results aren’t available. And earnings call transcripts aren’t available for this company either. So, while Armanino now has a $368 million market cap and Wall Street analyst coverage, there are risks here that don’t apply to other CPG stocks. Additionally, this is a low volume stock as well. So, it might be hard to exit from a position in this company if its growth plan doesn’t succeed.

Comparative Valuation for Armanino

Armanino’s new management team has provided results for the first 3 quarters of 2025, but some of this company’s financial results aren’t available right now. So, I can’t use the EV/EBITDA ratio here, and I’ll use the forward P/E ratio instead. According to Seeking Alpha, Armanino had a forward P/E of 21 after the market close on January 26. I’d compare this company to The Marzetti Company and McCormick (MKC), which also make sauces. On the same date, The Marzetti Company had a forward P/E of 24, and McCormick had a forward P/E of 20.

So, Armanino doesn’t appear to have much of a growth premium in comparison to other CPG companies that make sauces. Its forward yield of 1.69% is relatively low for a CPG stock, but that’s not as important for a growth stock. And again, this company has a high gross margin in comparison to its peers.

[object HTMLElement]

Estimating Armanino’s Value

Armanino didn’t provide full-year guidance for 2025, although the new CEO did say that this would be a record-breaking year in her January shareholder letter. Going by the results I’ve seen other CPG companies report recently, I’d expect lower growth in the fourth quarter. If I project that Armanino will report $19.1 million in revenue in Q4 2025, the company would report full-year revenue of $75.7 million. With a 24% profit margin, it would report 4Q income of $4.58 million and full-year income of $18.14 million.

Armanino has multiple growth drivers available in 2026, including sales to major fast-food chains and the potential for increased distribution. But I also think the company’s margins could drop. In the earnings report, the company said that it plans to invest in its business next year, and it may need to add more capacity to continue growing. In the 3Q earnings report, the CEO said, “We're reinvesting our gains into automation, process improvements, and talent to build the foundation for sustainable scale. While these initiatives, paired with ongoing macro pressures, may create near-term cost pressure, they are critical to positioning Armanino for long-term success.” So, I’ll project that the company’s revenue will rise 9% in 2026, but its net profit margin will fall from 24% to 23%. That would result in full-year revenue of $81.8 million and net income of $19.77 million.

With a 2026 P/E of 21, Armanino’s market cap would be $415 million. Armanino is also buying back stock, so I’d project that its share count will drop to 31.6 million shares in 2026 as well. That gives me a one-year price target of $13.13 per share. Armanino’s closing price was $11.87 on January 27, so my price target is 11% higher than the current price.

Several SA analysts recommended this stock at much lower prices. While it still has a buy rating, this company’s stock price has run up a lot recently. And the SA Quant score now says it’s a hold. While it still looks like Armanino’s price could rise further, it no longer looks like as much of a deal as it was earlier this year.

Conclusion

Armanino reported great quarterly results and is on track to post great results this year. I think it’s likely to continue growing rapidly in 2026 as well, even if its margins decline a bit. And right now, that seems like a real risk. Armanino’s CEO warned investors that the company expects short-term cost pressures that are likely to reduce its margins. Other CPG companies have also been reporting much weaker margins recently. Meanwhile, Armanino’s stock price has risen 54% in the last year, and it’s no longer trading at a large discount to other CPG stocks. So, I say Armanino is a hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Oceaneering: The Missing Link In Western Subsea Defense Capabilities
data.description:
Intro

Oceaneering International (NYSE:OII) represents a compelling deep-value investment opportunity as it moves beyond its traditional identity as a cyclical oilfield service provider to become a central pillar in Western subsea warfare and global industrial automation. The urgent mandate to protect Critical Undersea Infrastructure (CUI) from Russian and Chinese sabotage efforts creates a fully funded procurement priority for the U.S. government. This has been driven by the U.S. Navy's $350M "Seabed to Space" (S2ISR) framework and the launch of NATO's "Operation Baltic Sentry". Oceaneering is uniquely positioned to utilize its technology expertise in subsea robotics to solve the current gaps in our naval capabilities. This fast-growing segment of their business is complemented by a near 50% market share in the subsea robotics drill support market and hosts the world's largest fleet of 250 work-class ROVs, providing a high-margin cash floor that supports their expansion in the Western defense supply chain. Despite the Aerospace and Defense Technologies (ADTech) segment delivering 36% YoY growth in operating income, the market remains blind to this strategic pivot. Trading at a trailing P/E ratio of ~12.74x, OII represents a rare opportunity to capture a high-growth defense robotics play at a deep-value cyclical multiple.

Business Overview

As of Q3 FY2025, the company generated $743M in quarterly revenue. This was dominated by their Subsea Robotics (SSR) segment, which remains the cash cow, contributing $219M (~30% of revenue) with EBITDA margins of near 36% driven by their global fleet of Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs). OII functions as a service provider for these powerful hydraulic underwater robots that are capable of heavy intervention in subsea infrastructure and offshore oil rigs. For this segment, revenues are essentially flat YoY, but they have seen an uptick in revenue per day, which indicates their growing pricing power. This core operating business is complemented by the Offshore Projects Group (OPG), which instead involves OII taking ownership of the entire scope of work. They bring in their own equipment and crews in order to execute the task, whether that be subsea construction, oil well intervention, or inspection/repairs. This segment of their business generated ~$171M (~23% of revenue) at a 14% operating margin. This segment grew revenues by 16% YoY as they benefited from meaningful demand in the U.S. Gulf of Mexico. A much more focused and niche segment of their business comes from their Manufactured Products segment. In this part of the business, they manufacture some of the vital components in subsea robotics, such as umbilical cable systems and other high-end connectors/valves, which serve as key components in managing power on the seafloor. In this part of their business, they also have a portfolio of heavy industrial robots used for managing heavy payloads. This segment of their business generated ~$156M (~21% of total revenue) at an operating margin of 16% in Q3. This segment saw revenue growth of 9% YoY alongside a massive jump in operating income due to the renewed focus of their operations. The smallest segment of their business comes from the Integrity Management and Digital Solutions vertical. In this segment, OII provides 3D replicas and analysis of offshore assets in order to predict what parts need fixing without customers needing to shut down operations for an inspection. It contributes only ~9% of total revenue and operates at an operating margin of only ~4%. This segment had a revenue decline of 4% YoY, which was primarily attributed to a one-time non-cash charge and lower activity levels in certain regions. Their primary growth engine has now shifted towards the Aerospace & Defense segments, which generated ~$126M in revenue while growing at 27% YoY. This segment of their business now accounts for ~17% of total revenue and benefits from increased global defense spending. The current administration has shifted towards autonomous systems and has highlighted subsea defense as a critical area of concern. This has positioned OII to benefit from the global increase in defense spending by leveraging its unique expertise in underwater robotics. OII also holds numerous contracts with NASA, focusing on robotics arms and manufacturing space suits.

The Defense Pivot

Throughout 2024 and 2025, intelligence agencies observed unusual patterns of activity near critical trans-Atlantic fiber optic cables and energy pipelines. Incidents involving the Balticonnector pipeline and Nordic telecommunications cables demonstrated a capability and willingness to disrupt infrastructure in the "grey zone". These are actions that inflict massive economic damage but fall short of an armed attack. The challenge for NATO and the United States is that the ocean is far too vast to defend with manned ships. This creates a perfect opportunity for OII to apply its specialties in subsea robotics to these defense applications. The U.S. Navy has adopted a doctrine of Distributed Maritime Operations (DMO) and "Seabed to Space" integration. This doctrine requires satellites capable of monitoring surface vessel activity, as well as unmanned surface vessels that can act as communication gateways. Additionally, it necessitates the use of autonomous underwater vehicles and other seabed systems that can provide more specific monitoring services. Oceaneering's competency in remote operations and controlling subsea assets from onshore centers places them at the perfect intersection of these technologies. NATO's FY2026 military budget and the U.S. Navy's requests prioritize "unmanned vehicles" and "classified efforts" in the R&D portfolio. Specifically, the creation of NATO's "Undersea Infrastructure Coordination Cell" and the "Baltic Sentry" operation shows material proof of their shift towards subsea defense.

Oceaneering has a key competitive advantage in its ability to leverage its research and development across multiple customer bases. Their Freedom AUV product was funded largely through a Joint Industry Project with multiple energy majors. These commercial clients needed a vehicle capable of autonomous pipeline tracking, obstacle avoidance, and automatic docking to reduce their own operational costs. By the time the Navy needed a similar capability, Oceaneering had already achieved technological readiness with its commercial partnerships. This allowed the Navy to begin using Oceaneering products quickly without having to fund the initial stages of development. This "Spin-Off" of existing specialities and commercial technology allows OII to operate faster than the traditional primes that often require lengthy development cycles. This strategy mirrors the success of Anduril, which has eroded market share from the established primes by bypassing government-funded R&D in favor of deploying commercially mature, mission-ready systems. OII already supports a fleet of 250 work-class ROVs and has an established global supply chain for marine-grade components. The Defense Industrial Base is currently strained, and OII's ability to leverage its commercial supply chains could potentially be the difference in unit costs and lead times. Oceaneering's resident systems live on the seabed and dock at battery stations to recharge and upload data, only surfacing every few months for maintenance. For the Navy, this means that they can have constant surveillance of these critical cable routes at a fraction of the cost.

The Freedom AUV program is unlike conventional torpedo-shaped AUVs that rely on hydrodynamic lift and must maintain forward momentum. The Freedom AUV utilizes a specialized design that allows for hover capabilities and six-degrees of maneuverability. This is a critical capability for inspection and intervention tasks where the vehicle must stop and observe an anomaly. In early FY2025, the Defense Innovation Unit awarded Oceaneering a contract to build the Freedom AUV for the U.S. Navy. Management stated that this was the "largest initial contract value" in the history of this business segment. This contract involves not just the vehicles, but the establishment of an Onshore Remote Operation Center for the Navy. This moves OII from just a hardware vendor to an actual operational partner for the Navy.

OII has logged over 120,000 hours of remote operations for commercial energy clients since 2015. The Navy contract for this signifies the adoption of a similar service model in the defense segment, which should provide a high-margin revenue stream going forward. Once the Navy integrates OII's command and control infrastructure into its workflow, displacement will become difficult. The Navy intends to use Freedom for long-duration seabed monitoring. This operational deployment of these systems implies a significant revenue ramp for this segment. The unit cost is estimated to be in the multi-million dollar range. A fleet of even 10-20 units combined with operational servicing will create a substantial new revenue layer for this segment of their business going into FY2026.

Their autonomous systems are really the bread and butter in this segment, while their Submarine Rescue Systems provide a cash floor. Oceaneering currently manages the U.S Navy's Submarine Rescue Diving and Recompression System and the Pressurized Rescue Module System. These are critical missions, and recent contract wins extend these services through FY2029. In mid-2025, OII secured a $33M contract to manufacture support equipment for Virginia-class submarines. This integrates the company into the supply chain of the Navy's primary attack submarine platform. As the Navy ramps up production and prepares for the Columbia-class ballistic missile submarines, OII's role in maintenance support will grow proportionally.

On top of all of this, OII is a subcontractor for NASA with multiple contracts. OII is responsible for manufacturing the displays, tools, and vehicle interfaces for lunar missions. They are also a major subcontractor for the Neutral Buoyancy Laboratory. They provide the divers, equipment, and water treatment services in order to prepare astronauts for spacewalks. This business segment has very lumpy revenue recognition and is largely dependent on OII winning additional contracts for lunar tools and services.

Subsea Oil Infrastructure

While Oceaneering reports across five business segments, SSR and OPG account for the majority of their operating income and free cash flow. Their Subsea Robotics segment operates with high-margins and a dominant 60% global market share in drill support. Their Offshore Projects Group leverages their existing fleet to perform specific subsea intervention, installation, and inspection projects. Labor shortages and a further shift towards automation in the offshore drilling sector act as Macro tailwinds for Oceaneering's strategy. Their ability to centralize operations in remote centers becomes a strategic advantage as they can service more assets with fewer resources. Their Subsea Robotics segment is the crown jewel of their portfolio, sporting EBITDA margins in the mid-30s, significantly higher than the standard oilfield service company. This segment of their business includes the operation of Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs), Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs), and other survey/tooling services. Oceaneering currently has the world's largest fleet of work-class ROVs, with the fleet size currently standing near 250 fully operational systems. These systems are heavy-duty electro-hydraulic robots that are designed for depths up to 4,000 meters. Their Millennium Series is the heavy-lifter of their fleet. They have massive amounts of hydraulic horsepower, which allows them to be of great assistance for drilling support. Their Nexus series is designed for modularity, and it is often deployed for light drilling support and construction. Another signal of Oceaneering's ability to adapt its technology is in the Isurus work-class ROV.

Offshore wind farms are often located in very shallow waters with strong currents. OII adapted their ROVs with a streamlined design and additional thruster capacity, which allows the ROV to maintain stability and manipulate tools amidst strong tides. The Liberty E-ROV is a self-contained, battery-operated work-class ROV that stays on the seabed. A surface buoy then provides satellite communications and power for recharging. This E-ROV and the Freedom AUV system are the next steps in this business segment. By eliminating the need for continuous monitoring and support, these systems cut the costs significantly. Currently, ~63% of the fleet's activity is tied to drill support. These are typically long-term contracts and can be considered sticky revenue especailly considering their dominant market share. The other 37% of revenue is vessel-based and is more volatile to short-term activity. What's interesting is that recently, there has been a decoupling of pricing from utilization. In Q3 Fy2025, ROV fleet utilization declined slightly to 65% (from 68% in Q4 FY2024). On the other side of things, average revenue per day increased significantly to $11,254 (up 6% YoY). This trend indicates that the supply of these highly specified ROV systems is tighter than the headline numbers show. This pricing leverage will be the key part in further margin expansion in this business segment. Another key part in margin expansion in their business model is shifting towards the use of Onshore Remote operation Centers. These allow for pivots to control an ROV thousands of miles away with precision. The shift towards using this technology is very beneficial for margins as it eliminates the costs of personnel and logistics. Offshore drilling environments are also notoriously dangerous, so removing personnel from these areas reduces a company's exposure to high-risk incidents.

ROV Margins & Revenue

Metric Q4 FY2024 Q1 FY2025 Q2 FY2025 Q3 FY2025
Revenue ($M) 212.2 206.0 218.8 218.8
Operating Income 29.9% 28.9% 29.5% 29.7%
EBITDA Margin 36% 35% 35% 36%
Revenue Per Day ($) 10,784 10,788 11,265 11,254

Hidden Value In Manufactured Products

The recent divestiture of their Entertainment Systems business in mid-2025 marks a strategic pivot away from vanity projects and presents a refocusing towards core industrial products. While top-line revenue suffers from lumpy order intakes, operating margins have expanded meaningfully, doubling to 16% in Q3 FY2025. This signals their successful transition of this side of their business to higher-quality, high-margin revenue that is driven primarily by pricing power in their energy sub-segment. One part of this sub-segment is their Umbilical business. An umbilical is a complex, composite cable that connects surface vessels to subsea equipment. These are high-pressure tubes that are capable of carrying hydraulic fluid, other chemicals for maintenance operations, and power/data transmission. Oceaneering is a global leader in this market due to their cabailities in deepwater, high-pressure environments. Complimenting this product, they also sell connection systems for pipelines. Their Grayloc connectors are an industry standard for high-performance pipe connections, as they are extremely lightweight and reliable in these harsh environments. On the other hand, their rotator technologies segment produces control valves and chemical injection valves that allow material to be injected into oil wells. Management has explicitly stated that "pricing improvements in our Grayloc and Rotator product lines" are a primary driver in the margin expansion of this segment of their business. These components represent a tiny fraction of a project's total cost but are critical for safety and assurance, which makes the price inelastic. More importantly, their manufactured products segment encompasses a full portfolio of industrial robots that are uniquely suited for manufacturing logistics. These robots focus on moving car chassis, heavy pallets, and other industrial components in harsh factory environments. This portfolio of robots currently consists of three primary vehicle classes, each serving different purposes. The MaxMover CB D 2000 is an autonomous counterbalance forklift that has a payload capacity of 4,400 lbs. This forklift is equipped with 360 degrees of LiDAR sensors and elevated obstacle detection to see overhead structures, and is perfectly suited to heavy transporting needs.

Their UniMover O 600 is a device that works by sliding under carts or racks to lift them and is positioned to take up market share in healthcare, light manufacturing, and hospital logistics with a payload capacity of 1,322 lbs. This robot can spin in place and move laterally, which makes it capable of operating in very tight environments.

Finally, their CompactMover FOL U 1200 is a Fork-Over-Leg Stacker that is designed to work in environments in which a full forklift wouldn't fit. It has a payload capacity of 2,600 lbs and will be applied in narrow warehouses and end-of-line stacking.

OII utilizes natural feature navigation software, which uses local processing to map the environment dynamically. They also emphasized their software's ability to manage multiple types of robots simultaneously. OII faces intense competition from Seegrid, Rockwell Automation, and Mobile Industrial Robots due to their scale and focus on the niche. The main bottleneck between the competition and OII is in marketing and product reach. While OII may not win the volume of these automation orders, its MaxMover can handle a higher payload compared to its peers. Their growth strategy will need to be centered around deep integration of their systems and more skewed to heavier industrial applications for them to be able take up market share. Growth in this sub-segment is expected to really accelerate in late FY2026 to early FY2027 as pilot programs of warehouse automation convert to full deployment at scale. This validation phase of these technologies will be lengthy, but once adoption starts to take pace, the rate at which it will grow will be exponential. We effectively treat this as a free call option that comes with the stock, as they have their foot in the door, but still need to prove themselves in the industry for it to really be considered in valuation. The main value in this segment for now is their pricing power in energy segments, which is expected to allow them to maintain healthy margins.

Financial Snapshot

In Q3 FY2025, OII reported revenue of $742.9M (up 9.3% YoY) alongside net income increasing to $71.3M (up 73% YoY). Their balance sheet remains strong with a cash position of $516M compared to their total long-term debt position of $486M. This net cash position is often rare for a capital-intensive oilfield services company, and this allowed them to repurchase $10.1M of shares in Q3 FY2025 alone. For FY2026, management gave guidance for consolidated EBITDA in the range of $390M-$440M. Margins continue to expand with gross margin increasing to 20.6% compared to 19.3% in Q3 FY2024. More interestingly, operating income surged 21% YoY, with operating margins now approaching 11.6%, which has been largely driven by the diversification of low-margin businesses and services. In Q3 FY2025, they generated $77M in FCF, which allows them to continue funding the ramp-up of ADtech & continue share repurchase activity. Their financial stability is anchored to their net cash position and strong free cash flow, which gives the company the firepower to continue to grow in the niches that they operate in.

Model

In our model, we assume ADTech revenue to grow ~25% annually as their contracts for the Freedom AUV and the establishment of an OROC start to materialize. The Navy's FY2026 budget sets priorities for unmanned technology, which directly funds OII's niche. Recent data showed a decoupling of utilization and pricing in their Subsea Robotics segment. Growth in this vertical is assumed to be ~7% annually as customers continue to pay a premium for OII's remote-operations and reliability. For their offshore projects segment, we expect moderate revenue growth of ~5.5% driven by continued maintenance work in the Gulf of Mexico and West Africa. We can expect revenues from this segment to be lumpy from quarter to quarter due to the types of projects they take on in this vertical. Revenue growth for their manufactured products segment is set at a conservative ~2.5% annually, given their low book-to-bill ratio of 0.82. However, margins are expected to expand materially as they continue to burn off their older low-margin backlog. We expect ~3% annual growth from their integrity management systems business segment, given their long-term contracts in this vertical.

Projections Per Segment

Segment Projected FY2026 Revenue Growth
ADTech +25.0%
Subsea Robotics +7%
Manufactured products +2.5%
Offshore Projects +5.5%
IMDS +3.0%

Historically, OII has struggled in the earlier parts of the year with management explicitly guiding for "seasonally lower activity levels in our energy-focused businesses" for Q1 FY2026. Our model reflects this, creating a very conservative baseline for valuation in the case that they outperform.

Overall Projections

Metric Q3 FY2025 (actual) Q4 FY2025 Q1 FY2026 Q2 FY2026 Q3 FY2026 YoY Growth
Total Revenue ($M) 743.0 705.5 692.0 755.0 807.5 +8.7%
Gross Margin (%) 20.6 19.8 19.2 20.5 22.0% +140 bps
Operating Income ($M) 86.5 72.0 63.5 89.0 106.5 +23.1%
Operating Margin (%) 11.6 10.2 9.2 11.8 13.2 +160 bps
EBITDA ($M) 111.0 97.5 90.5 116.0 134.5 +21.2%
EBITDA Margin (%) 14.9 13.8 13.1 15.4 16.7 +180 bps
Net Income ($M) 71.3 47.2 40.6 60.8 73.5 +3.1%
Diluted EPS ($) 0.71 0.47 0.41 0.61 0.73 +2.8%

It is important to note that Q3 FY2025 benefited from a one-time discrete tax benefit, which drove net income much higher than estimates. This is the reason why their YoY EPS growth doesn't look that impressive. On the other side of things, EBITDA is projected to grow 21% YoY alongside EBITDA margins moving to 16.7% from 14.9% based on their immense operational leverage and continued shift towards high-margin products/services.

Valuation

Considering our four quarters of projections for EBITDA, we can get a TTM estimate of $438.5M. Currently, OII has an enterprise value of ~$3.30B, which gives us a forward EV/EBITDA of 7.53x. It is difficult to find direct peers to OII due to its diversified and unique business model. We can use Coda Octopus Group (NASDAQ:CODA) for a view into the potential re-rating upon the ramp-up of OII's ADTech division. Coda is a pure-play bet on subsea defense, as they provide niche hardware/software that enables AUVs and human divers to visualize subsea environments. Their technology has wide applications to defense and can serve as a comparison for OII's eventual re-rating. Currently, CODA trades at a FWD EV/EBITDA of 16.68x. If we assume a modest re-rating to a 10x EV/EBITDA multiple, given the fact that OII is not a pure-play, this would imply a price target of ~$40, which represents a potential upside of around 37% from current levels.

Risks & What To Monitor

While the investment case for OII is strong, many risks come with the investment. A large portion of the thesis revolves around a re-rating, given the fast-growing ADTech business segment. If the U.S. Navy delays its investments in subsea defense technologies, then the revenue growth from this vertical could be largely stunted. If this were to happen, their growth story would rely entirely on its Subsea Robotics segment, which is not enough to warrant a valuation rerating. There are also seasonality risks that come with their OPG segment, as a harsh winter could delay project starts and push revenue recognition into Q3/Q4. The book-to-bill ratio of 0.82 in their manufacturing products segment is also a concern, but this can also be a side-effect of their refocusing of operations. It will be important to monitor this going forward to ensure that orders start to pick up. As we move further into FY2026, it will be important to monitor SSR fleet utilization and ensure it does not drop below 60%. It would also be very positive to see ADTech's book-to-bill ratio expanding, and OII named as a integator or subcontractor for more Navy projects. Lastly, seeing relative outperformance in OII compared to the VanEck Oil Services ETF (NYSE:OIH) would be a positive indication that the market is assigning value to their defense applications.

Conclusion

Oceaneering is a company that currently stands at a critical inflection point as they transition into being a key supplier in Western Subsea Warfare. Investors are currently assigning OII with a cyclical oilfield service multiple, even though their defense business is growing at >20% annually. As they continue to scale this part of their business and potentially win additional contracts with the U.S. Navy, the company will be re-rated to a higher multiple. Alongside this, their Subsea Robotics segment provides a strong cash floor for continued R&D in this new vertical. Given the U.S. Navy's "Seabed to Space" mandate and the persistent adoption of autonomous technologies, the demand for OII's expertise should only continue to grow. With a FWD EV/EBITDA of ~7.5x, OII offers an asymmetric opportunity as they continue to scale and grow into their positioning within the United States subsea defense supply chain.

data.title: Centene: Downgrading On Timing, Not Fundamentals
data.description:
After UnitedHealth's (UNH) yesterday Q4 earnings release, the entire industry suffered a big panic sell-off. Despite Centene's (CNC) exposure to governmental healthcare programs is not as significant as UNH's, it is still notable, and the stock also saw a double-digit share price decline yesterday. I have already gained more than 40% on CNC since my late August decision to buy this stock and I think that it has solid long-term potential due to structural factors. However, we see that due to adverse political and regulatory developments, the entire sector is at risk of short-term volatility. Due to all potential near-term headwinds, I am not buying more shares even after yesterday's dip. Therefore, I am downgrading CENE to a 'Hold'.

CNC Q4 earnings preview

Centene releases its earnings next Friday, February 6, which makes it a risky investment at the moment because we are highly uncertain about how the management's guidance can be affected by almost invisible 0.09% expected increase in reimbursement rates for Medicare Advantage payers. Given challenging Medicare and Medicaid funding environment, it is difficult to forecast Centene's management's approach to mitigate the risk. If they decide to shift toward prioritizing margin over membership volume like UNH did, there might be a notable temporary dip in the projected revenue growth. Therefore, the management's guidance and forward-looking statements during next week's earnings call is extremely difficult to forecast.

Uncertainty around revenue guidance brings serious risks for the share price movement after earnings because recent revenue revisions from Wall Street analysts were mostly positive, meaning that expectations of the public regarding topline growth sustainability over the long-term were improving. High expectations together with increasing uncertainty regarding revenue guidance is a setup that can lead to big disappointment for investors.

On the other hand, recent challenges related to Medicare and Medicaid funding are unlikely to affect Q4 performance. Due to Centene's extremely strong earnings surprise history, I expect the company to be at least in line with Wall Street's Q4 projections of $192.9 billion in sales and $2.04 in EPS. Moreover, the company usually delivers quite large revenue and EPS surprises, which means that in reality CNC might notably outperform consensus estimates.

However, despite CNC being a value stock, I think that uncertainty about the company's financial performance in 2026 and 2027 is likely to outweigh a potential solid double Q4 earnings beat. Moreover, I think that investors who bought CNC during the August dip at below $30 per share might be eager to book 30%-40% profits amid the likely near-term volatility. Moreover, the political uncertainty is likely to remain high for the next few years because President Trump's administration has several initiatives to transform rules and regulations for the healthcare sector because it constitutes a lion's portion of the U.S. GDP. Since Trump's administration is seeking ways to optimize the budget, it is highly likely that they will focus on cutting costs in the largest sectors of the economy like healthcare as each initiative will have significant economic effect.

Finally, even after today's big selloff, Wall Street analysts project a tiny 5.7% upside potential as the consensus target price is circa $44. Buying before Q4 earnings to potentially gain a 5%-6% near-term gain doesn't look like a great deal given that there might be a post-earnings selloff if the management's guidance disappoints investors.

Factors against rating downgrade

Centene's valuation remains extremely attractive, if we look at the company's valuation ratios. Most of the multiples are significantly lower than the sector and CNC's historical averages. Some of the ratios are particularly attractive for a value company. For example, CNC's forward P/FCF ratio is just 6.33%. It is massively lower than the sector (60% difference) and than the company's last five-years' average (42% difference).

Investors might buy the current dip aggressively given CNC's extremely low valuation ratios across the board because there are no apparent fundamental flaws other than the current uncertainty. CNC has a robust record of success even during crisis times (the Great Recession, COVID-19 pandemic), stellar profitability compared to the sector, and a healthy balance sheet. Despite all the volatility and uncertainty the company faced in 2025, its tangible book value per share increased by more than 30% during the first three quarters of the year. The management's prudent capital allocation means that the company has quite low debt levels, which makes it well-equipped to weather even prolonged storms.

Finally, secular trends remain favorable for the industry as there is a favorable demographic setup. The population of the United States is aging, and the U.S. Census Bureau forecasts that all Baby Boomers will be ages 65 or older by 2030. Looking beyond the end of current decade, the population of Americans ages 65 and older is projected to increase substantially, from 58 million in 2022 to 82 million by 2050. It means that the total addressable market has potential to expand by approximately 40% if we assume that there is strong correlation between the market size and the number of elderly people in the economy. As one of the largest players in the industry with solid reach and reputation, CNC is strategically aligned to benefit from this secular trend.

Conclusion

Due to the extremely high level of uncertainty regarding the management's guidance and forward-looking comments, I prefer to sit on the fence this time. It is extremely difficult to forecast what the management's strategic response to adverse Medicare-related headlines might be, which might lead to unfavorable post-earnings volatility. However, since CNC's multiples remain extremely low even after the 40%+ rally since my last call and favorable secular trend of aging U.S. population, I am not ready to sell this fundamentally strong company. Therefore, CNC is a 'Hold' at the moment.

data.title: Popular: Solid Q4 2025 Earnings And Undemanding Valuation
data.description:
Introduction

Shares in Puerto Rico-focused bank Popular (BPOP) are off to a solid start in 2026, boosted by strong Q4 2025 financial results. While the impressive 40.6% Y/Y increase in quarterly EPS was partially the result of a reversal of a FDIC special assessment provision, Popular's underlying performance remains sound. As such, I confirm my previous Buy rating on Popular, with my bullish outlook driven by:

  • An undemanding 10.6x trailing P/E multiple for BPOP, representing a circa 20% discount to regional bank peers.
  • Positive 2026 outlook, with a circa 11% EPS increase likely to represent a healthy mix between organic growth and share repurchases.
  • A strong capital position and low loan to deposit ratio, allowing Popular to remain aggressive on deposit pricing, thus achieving an attractive net interest margin.

There have been no major changes in the company's structure since I last covered Popular. As such, the Company Overview section in my previous article provides a good snapshot of Popular for readers not familiar with the bank, while the latest Q4 2025 results presentation is available here.

Q4 2025 Results Overview

Popular reported EPS of $3.53/share in Q4 2025, up 40.6% Y/Y. A small part of the increase was driven by share buybacks throughout the year, with shares outstanding down roughly 6.3% Y/Y. In addition, a one-off reversal of a FDIC special assessment provision contributed $0.15/share to quarterly EPS, or roughly 5.9% of the Y/Y EPS growth.

With these caveats in mind, Popular's earnings report was quite solid. The company navigated Fed rate cuts quite well, reporting a 0.1% Q/Q increase in the net interest margin to 3.61%, while credit quality continued to improve, as evidenced by lower non-performing loans and net charge-offs Q/Q. Non-interest income rose 1% Y/Y, in line with the 1% increase in operating expenses.

Tangible book value stood at $82.65/share, up 4.5% Q/Q. The return on tangible common equity [ROTCE] stood at 14.39% in the quarter, up 3.17% Y/Y. The CET1 capital ratio remained robust at 15.72%, albeit declining 0.31% Y/Y, driven by growth in risk-weighted assets.

All in all, despite a notable one-off factor contributing to EPS growth in the quarter, BPOP's quarterly financial results were quite solid, as evidenced by a positive market reaction to the earnings report, with BPOP rising 6.29% on the day.

Positive 2026 Outlook

Popular unveiled a bullish 2026 outlook, with loan growth seen at 3-4% (2025: 6.2%), which would allow for a 5-7% increase in net interest income (2025: +11.3%). Credit card loans only account for 3.3% of Popular's total loan book, indicating a limited impact on profitability even if we see some sort of implementation of the recently proposed 10% cap on credit card interest rates.

Operating expenses are seen up 3% Y/Y, while net charge-offs are expected at 0.55-0.70% of loans, somewhat above the 0.45-0.60% quarterly range we have seen in 2025.

To sum up, I think a ROTCE comparable to the 13.04% reached in 2025 remains achievable for Popular, with the company taking advantage of its vast investment securities portfolio to remain aggressive on deposit pricing, while organic loan growth drives net interest income growth.

Popular Valuation

The bank achieved EPS of $12.31/share in 2025, indicating that the company trades at a trailing P/E of only 10.6x even after the strong start to 2026. Analysts have penciled in EPS of $13.66/share for 2026, which seems somewhat optimistic in light of the one-off effects that boosted 2025 results, as well as the outlook for marginally higher credit costs.

I should quickly note however that Popular exhibits a low 0.24x dividend payout ratio, hence I would think the anticipated 11% EPS increase in 2026 also reflects the effect of ongoing share repurchases. As the company spent $502 million on share buybacks in 2025, a comparable amount this year would be enough to boost EPS by roughly 6%.

As such, you can view Popular's 2026 EPS growth as a healthy mix between organic growth in the mid-single-digits, as well as capital return via share repurchases in light of the business's undemanding valuation.

I calculate that Popular's regional bank peers held in the iShares U.S. Regional Banks ETF (IAT) trade at a trailing P/E of 13.5x, indicating that Popular's valuation represents a meaningful circa 20% discount relative to the overall sector.

All in all, notwithstanding risks which I will discuss shortly, I believe Popular's sound financial performance, positive 2026 outlook, undemanding valuation, and strong capital position present an attractive investment opportunity, confirming my previous Buy rating on the shares.

Risks

As with any bank, a significant downturn in the economy remains a key risk in the investment case. Since 80% of Popular's assets are allocated to Puerto Rico, monitoring the U.S. territory's economic outlook remains a key point of attention for investors. With the unemployment rate holding steady at 5.7% in recent months, an immediate deterioration in credit quality remains unlikely.

Investors will also pay close attention to how BPOP navigates ongoing Fed rate cuts and their effect on the company's net interest margin. In 2025 deposits grew by $1.3 billion, below the $2.2 billion increase in loans. This indicates that Popular's aggressive pricing on deposits is resulting in an incremental increase in the loan to deposit ratio. Still, at only 59.3%, Popular's loan to deposit ratio remains a non-issue in the short term, allowing the bank to reap the benefits of higher net interest income. The effect of high-margin loan growth is offset by declines in the low-margin securities portfolio.

Conclusion

Popular shares are off to a strong start in 2026, contributing to the impressive 29% total return achieved over the past year. Even so, BPOP remains cheap relative to direct regional bank peers, with the bank's trailing P/E of 10.6x representing a roughly 20% discount to the overall sector.

Analysts expect a circa 11% increase in EPS for 2026. I estimate that approximately half of the gain should come from organic revenue growth, while the other half will likely stem from ongoing share repurchases.

Amid solid Q4 2025 financials and a positive 2026 outlook, I see no major red flags for BPOP for the rest of 2026. As such, I confirm my Buy rating on BPOP.

While the current 2026 outlook is quite rosy, a significant downturn in Puerto Rico's economy remains a key risk in the investment case. In the long term, an increase in the loan to deposit ratio may also put pressure on Popular's net interest margin, although the sizable investment securities portfolio indicates no immediate risk on this front.

Thank you for reading.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Twenty One Capital: Wen Business, Ser?
data.description:
In late April 2025, Twenty One Capital (XXI) announced a proposed combination with SPAC entity Cantor Equity Partners, Inc. (CEP). Backed by stablecoin giant Tether and Softbank Group (SFTBY), Twenty One Capital was created to be yet another Bitcoin (BTC-USD) DAT focused on growing BTC holdings through Bitcoin-related business models.

It has not gone well, to say the least.

After exploding nearly 500% in the immediate sessions following Twenty One Capital's announced SPAC merger, the stock has fallen by over 85% in nearly a straight line over the last nine months. Furthermore, since beginning trading as XXI in December, the stock is already down almost 30%. Essentially, almost every shareholder who has purchased XXI since the merger is likely underwater. For those watching from the sidelines, could this selloff have created an opportunity in XXI?

Twenty One KPIs and Operations

When Twenty One initially launched, the company was quite adamant that 'Bitcoin Per Share' was one of the primary ways to view the business's success. The metric was literally front and center on the company's website. This is what it said in its introductory press release:

As part of its launch, Twenty One will introduce two key performance metrics, to reflect its Bitcoin-denominated capital structure and Bitcoin-focused mindset.

Those two KPIs were BPS, or Bitcoin Per Share, and BRR, or Bitcoin Return Rate. Fully diluted BPS was expected to grow over time with BRR reflecting the growth rate of BPS. This is what that same website looks like today:

More recently, the company has seemingly abandoned BPS entirely. Which would relegate BRR as essentially useless as well. Thus, there is not currently a suggested metric by which to measure Twenty One's performance. And this probably shouldn't be all that surprising since the company doesn't appear to have any operating business whatsoever.

Twenty One Capital's co-founder and CEO is notable Bitcoin maximalist Jack Mallers. Through the years, Mallers has been a frequent guest on Bitcoin podcasts and an influential speaker at Bitcoin conferences. From my vantage, Mallers has a reputation as being outspoken, possibly even to a fault, as he generally does not mince words or hold back his opinions. I personally see him as a bit of a Michael Saylor-type figure within the Bitcoin community. Though unlike Saylor, Mallers has contributed to the Bitcoin ecosystem through a functioning business via his other company.

Jack Mallers is also the founder and CEO of the peer-to-peer Bitcoin Lightning Network-based payments company Strike, which by itself can potentially raise concerns about Mallers' focus on XXI. Though to be clear, I don't personally have that concern about what will essentially be two different Bitcoin-based financial services businesses. On one of Mallers' recent podcast streams, he was asked about the lack of an announced business segment for XXI and reiterated that the company is working on developing a revenue stream and will provide those details when it can.

Bitcoin Holdings and Share Breakdown

Despite the lack of anything resembling a tangible business, Twenty One is the third largest corporate holder of Bitcoin on the planet after Strategy (MSTR) and MARA Holdings (MARA). Twenty One reportedly holds 43,514 BTC. This Bitcoin was raised through several different methods. First, Tether and Bitfinex contributed 31,500 BTC prior to the merger closing. Twenty One was also active in raising hundreds of millions in capital through 1% convertible notes due in 2030 as well as through PIPE financing deals.

As of January 2nd, there are 346.5 million shares outstanding, and the capitalization breakdown shakes out as follows:

Equity Capitalization Summary Shares %
Twenty One Class A members 304.9 88.0%
Public Shareholders 9.5 2.7%
Sponsor 4.3 1.3%
Equity PIPE Investors 27.9 8.0%

Source: Twenty One, S-1, shares in millions

As has typically been the case from what I've seen with these kinds of deals, the carryover shareholders from the original SPAC equity make up a very small percentage of the total outstanding shares. More importantly, Tether and Bitfinex combine to own more than half the outstanding shares, which means Twenty One is a "controlled company" per NYSE rules.

mNAV Calculation XXI
Common outstanding 1/2/26 346,548,153
Share Price $8.50
Market Capitalization $2,945,659,301
Bitcoin Valuation $3,877,097,400
Basic mNAV* 0.76
Diluted mNAV* 0.84

Source: Twenty One, S-1, analyst's calculation based on $89.1k BTC price

For the diluted mNAV estimate, I used the 35 million shares on offer from the company's recently filed S-1. The company has $486.5 million in non-current liabilities, which are due to Twenty One's convertible notes. But even when assuming that dilution, XXI trades at an mNAV of just 0.84. We've seen the compression of mNAVs in the DAT space continue to be a problem in recent months. While an mNAV under 1 takes away the incentive for management to dilute shareholders to acquire more coins, it does force these companies to generate shareholder value through the execution of an actual business strategy. And right now, Twenty One doesn't have one yet. At least not one that is being shared publicly.

Risks

While XXI might seem like a decent lotto ticket 'buy' at a 16% discount to diluted mNAV, there are certainly still risks to consider. The first of which, Twenty One, is essentially just a Bitcoin holding company as currently constructed. Thus, it will likely have the kind of volatility that we've come to expect from DAT stocks over the last year. While it is a holding company of an asset that is designed to be held in self-custody, Twenty One does utilize a third party for custodial services. Which introduces third-party risk to the stock in the event of a broader crypto market meltdown.

Additionally, Tether and Bitfinex own more than 50% of XXI voting power collectively. Meaning retail common stock shareholders have no power as a voting block. As a controlled company, Twenty One can exempt itself from an independent majority on the Board of Directors. Which may not necessarily be a good thing for minority shareholders. Finally, if you're not bullish on Bitcoin as a base case, there's essentially no reason to be long XXI shares today. But there are numerous alternative ways one can express a bullish Bitcoin thesis without introducing leadership risks from a public company.

Closing Summary

I don't think I can justify calling XXI a 'buy' until we have an actual business plan. Based on the limited filings the market has from the company so far, it appears as though the early operational focus for Twenty One will include Bitcoin accumulation and Bitcoin content generation. Accumulating BTC at an mNAV discount likely isn't happening. And the content generation angle doesn't seem all that lucrative from where I sit. Per the December 12th 10-Q:

Twenty One plans to create and monetize high-quality educational content through channels such as subscriptions, licensing fees for enterprises, and sponsored partnerships, which are expected to contribute to its revenue streams.

This type of business is not a good enough reason to buy XXI shares in my view. Content generation is already a very crowded area, especially within the Bitcoin community. And given the company's quick pivot from BPS as a KPI to no longer seeing it as an important measuring stick, it seems to me that Twenty One is still trying to figure out what it actually is. I don't know that I'd be selling under $9 per share. But it's tough to be interested in yet another Bitcoin speculative arb opportunity when the precious metals market is on fire to the degree that it is. Based on the opportunity cost alone, I think XXI is a 'hold.'

data.title: BAB: I See Less Value In Taxable Munis Now (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Main Thesis & Background

The purpose of this article is to evaluate the Invesco Taxable Municipal Bond ETF (BAB) as an investment option at its current market price. The fund is managed by Invesco, and it is designed to "track the performance of US dollar-denominated taxable municipal debt publicly issued by US states and territories." This is a passive ETF, which is a contrast to most of the funds I normally review in this space, which are leveraged CEFs.

I wrote about BAB in early 2025, and I had a bullish take on the fund. I believed munis overall represented value, taxable and tax-exempt alike, and thought BAB would deliver a positive return. Looking back, I was right, as this ETF beat out cash and other fixed-income options that were more conservative, as it offered a return near 7% in just under a year:

As we push closer to February, I am taking another look at all the sectors I cover, and taxable munis are included in this exercise. Given BAB's nice run over the past year, I thought it would be wise to consider if more gains are likely or if better opportunities exist elsewhere.

After review, I don't think BAB's returns will be very strong in the coming months, and I am downgrading my outlook to "hold." One key reason is I see stronger value in the tax-exempt muni sector, and that is impacting my outlook for taxable munis (which BAB owns). While this opinion may not be shared by those with lower tax burdens, I am not fortunate enough to be in that camp, and I don't see a compelling reason to buy BAB for the year ahead. I will explain why in greater detail below.

Tax-Exempt Munis Offer More Competitive Yields

The first topic right off the bat for me is income. When one is considering municipal bonds as a whole, it is likely because they want a defensive portion of their portfolio to also be income-generating. This is true for both the taxable and tax-exempt sectors, and BAB certainly accomplishes that. But this is where the fund falls short for me at current valuations. At present, the fund is offering an annualized yield in the 4% range:

January Distribution Current Share Price (BAB) Annualized Yield (%)
$.09163 $27.27 4.03%

Source: Invesco

Now, this is not "bad." It beats out what cash is offering, and it is comparable to IG-rated corporate bonds. (Some corporates yield less, others more, but in aggregate it is similar). But I am a working professional in an elevated federal tax bracket. For me, this often means tax-exempt munis offer the better value proposition, and that does indeed ring true today.

To illustrate, let us consider the tax-exempt yield curve at the moment. We can see that the average muni issue is yielding around 3.5%. This is less than 4%, of course. But when tax savings are factored in, the after-tax yield is likely higher:

I say "likely" because it depends on one's individual tax situation. If one has a small tax burden (or none at all), then taxable munis may hold the better value. But that is not the case for my household nor for many others on this platform, I would imagine. It only takes a .5% boost in tax savings for tax-exempt munis to rival BAB's current yield, and many of us will save much more than that.

This is central to my downgrade to "hold." I see better value elsewhere within the municipal bond space. This doesn't mean the argument will hold true for everyone, but for those in tax brackets ranging 20% or higher, I would imagine one could do better branching into the tax-exempt sector and away from BAB. That is the strategy I will be pursuing in 2026.

The Environment Is Ripe For Hedges

Before digging into the specifics of BAB, I do want to take a moment to reflect on the macro-backdrop. From last year through today, it has been a market to remember!
