Yes, assets are up almost across the board, but we have seen our share of volatility and uncertainty with regard to trade wars, tariffs, and potential military conflicts. Through these ups and downs, we have seen an environment develop where taxable munis have performed quite strongly:

As you can see, taxable munis have been an outperformer within the fixed-income realm over the past year. This could continue - so one may argue with my "hold" rating and decide a "buy" rating is still warranted. I, personally, am a bit of a contrarian in that I don't see any particular bond sector out-performing for a long time. I think, given that investors prefer yield in this space, they will rotate to the higher-yielding plays and thus smooth out performance over time (as one sector lags, their yield should theoretically become more attractive, all other things being equal).

But there is no denying taxable munis, and BAB by extension, have done well, and that could certainly continue. As geopolitical and trade risks remain elevated as we push into 2026, equity hedges and sectors like munis that offer stability could continue to generate investor attention.

The bottom line here is I am saying BAB and the underlying taxable muni sector have performed well for good reason, and an overly bearish take on this sector is not warranted. While I see better value elsewhere, I still expect positive, albeit modest, returns from this ETF, and I say this to help manage expectations as we move forward from here.

California's Bonds More Economically Sensitive

I will now turn the focus to BAB specifically. One item that caught my eye is that this fund is heavily concentrated towards California. While this is a national fund and not state-specific, almost one-quarter of the fund's assets originated from the Golden State:

In fairness, I have often had a positive view on California muni bonds, as my followers know. But that comes with a caveat. I generally suggest those for residents of that state who also incur a relatively high tax burden. Under that scenario, taking on the concentration risk of single-state munis can make a lot of sense to maximize the tax savings. That comes with inherent risks, but I generally view those risks as manageable.

But that story may not be the case for out-of-state residents. This is especially true for taxable munis since owners of these securities don't enjoy a tax exemption. So it strikes me that investors should favor other attributes, such as diversification, given the lacking tax advantages.

This should help explain why I view this concentration as more of a headwind than in other articles I have written. My focus on California munis in tax-exempt, single-state funds for the right buyer is generally bullish. But that is not the dynamic at play here. Further, readers should understand that California's tax system is heavily skewed towards personal income taxes. This makes the state's tax collections more economically sensitive, as these revenues will decline when the stock market and/or the economy take a hit:

The takeaway for me is this has led to a concentration in economically sensitive revenue streams for the state of California. Thus, the revenue sources that back California's muni bonds are reliant on a strong job market and/or a strong stock market. If we see declines in either, that will have an outsized impact on state finances and could hurt the credit quality of the underlying securities in BAB's portfolio.

For now, the job market and stock market are strong, so this isn't an immediate concern. But things can change quickly, and my outlook on the macro level is very cautious. This should help explain why I am less bullish on a fund like BAB than I was last year.

BAB Could Beat Corporate Bonds

My final point has a more upbeat tenor. This has to do with the credit quality of BAB's holdings. I noted above how the California bonds are sensitive to the economy, but the economy is in good shape right now. Further, that isn't the only state in the portfolio. The net result is BAB has a quality-focused holdings list, and the majority of its securities are rated triple-A or double-A:

I want to highlight this for two reasons. One, this is a pretty safe fund from a credit perspective. So while I am not a buyer here, this isn't because the fund is too risky. It is perhaps that many may want to take on more risk than it offers!

Two, while I see better value in tax-exempt munis at present, the reverse argument is true when we compare taxable munis against comparable corporate bonds (which are also taxable). Securities rated double-AA, which are the bulk of BAB's portfolio, currently have a yield advantage against comparably rated corporate bonds:

This again supports at least a "hold" view for now. In a sense, I see very little reason to buy corporate bonds when taxable munis have stronger income potential. Of course, as mentioned multiple times, tax-exempt munis win overall, but if one ruled out that sector and is looking into the taxable realm, then munis could be the better play. This is why I will keep BAB on my radar even though I am not a buyer at this exact moment.

Bottom Line

BAB has pumped out a reasonable return over the past year, but I now see better opportunities elsewhere. The fund's income stream is inferior to tax-exempt munis for my personal tax situation, and this fund is also heavily exposed to California debt, which is not a state I reside in (I live in North Carolina), and it is also more economically sensitive than the average US state. For these reasons and others discussed in this review, I am downgrading my view to "hold" at this time. I would suggest to my followers they approach any new positions very selectively for now.

data.title: Dingdong (Cayman) Limited: Why It Could Be Better Off Teaming Up
data.description:
Dingdong (Cayman) Limited (DDL), an e-commerce company operating in China, has done well lately, with the stock way up in the past two months or so after struggling for quite some time. However, the stock has been meandering around and struggling to find directions more recently, which raises the possibility the stock could give up the recent gains if nothing firm shows up to justify the recent appreciation in the stock, especially with DDL’s future in the grocery business looking difficult.

What DDL Does

DDL is an e-commerce company in China. Specifically, DDL operates an online platform where customers can place orders for and delivery of mostly fresh groceries and other daily necessities. As a consequence, DDL has a lot of growth potential due to the size of the market it operates in, with roughly 1.4 billion people in China, almost all of whom need groceries in some way. This translates into a market worth trillions.

On the other hand, the grocery market has many players, and competition is tough, both online and offline. The market leader is Meituan, which, with $47B in sales in the last completed fiscal year (FY2024), is much larger than DDL. Other players worth mentioning are Alibaba (BABA) and Pinduoduo (PDD), which are even larger. Note that these companies have a much larger focus than the grocery business DDL specializes in.

Barriers to entry in groceries are low, and it is difficult to differentiate since everything, or almost everything, for sale can also be sold by someone else. The main difference between competitors often comes down to price, which may be somewhat higher or lower. Margins are very low as a result, which means profits are hard to come by.

DDL is not much different in that regard, with, for instance, a net profit margin currently at slightly more than one percent, but it is worth noting how DDL has been able to greatly improve the bottom line in recent years. DDL once racked up big losses, but it has been able to improve profitability in each of the last several years since it went public on the NYSE in June 2021 with an IPO price of $23.50 per ADS.

In the last three completed fiscal years, DDL showed much progress by posting a net profit of $0.18 per ADS in FY2024 after posting net losses of $0.55 per ADS in FY2022 and $0.06 per ADS in FY2023. DDL is profitable, and it has been for the last seven quarters in terms of GAAP and even longer in terms of non-GAAP.

DDL posted TTM GAAP net income of $38.88M, or $0.16 per ADS, which accords it a P/E ratio of 17x, with the stock last closing at $2.76 per ADS. This includes earnings of $0.05 per ADS on revenue of $935.9M in the most recent or Q3 FY2025 report. GMV was a bit higher at $1,021.7M, an increase of 0.1% YoY. Still, DDL needed to post $3.33B in TTM sales to get it done, which gives you a sense of what kind of market the grocery market is in China.

DDL Has Fallen Greatly, Although It Bounced Recently

However, while DDL made major strides in terms of improving the bottom line in the years following the IPO, the stock itself has not benefited much from the progress made. On the contrary, the stock trades at a fraction of what it went for at the IPO due to the decline in the stock price. This decline continued for much of 2025, with DDL going down until December, when the stock started to rally on elevated volume.

There were no obvious catalysts to be found as to what triggered the rally, nor did anything from DDL hit the news, but the rally started not long after the stock hit a 52-week low of $1.65 per ADS in November 2025. The rally quickly took the stock to as high as $3.41 per ADS on December 26, or twice as much, but DDL has not gone any higher and instead has pulled back since then. The chart below shows how the stock has come to life after a long decline.

It’s possible that bargain buyers decided to step in after the stock fell by as much as it did, which resulted in DDL being available at reduced valuations and which could have inspired enough to get in on what may have seemed like a relative bargain. However, it is also worth noting how in the chart above the rally in the stock coincided with a large increase in trading volume. This suggests that there were buyers that seemed to want to accumulate shares in a hurry.

It may be speculation since there is no way to know for sure what motivated each and every single one of these buyers, but one possibility worth exploring is that DDL could become an acquisition target, whether justified or not, since DDL is available at vastly reduced valuations after the decline in the stock price of the past few years.

DDL has a market cap of just $598M after the stock closed at $2.76 per ADS on January 26, or an enterprise value of just $415M after adjusting for net cash. So putting in an offer does not have to come at too high a price, with DDL valued at just 0.18x TTM sales of $3.33B. Adding DDL is something an existing e-commerce company could conceivably use to achieve, for instance, greater scale and efficiency.

Why Adding DDL May or May Not Make Sense for Another E-commerce Company

If DDL were to become part of a larger e-commerce entity, then that could unlock value. For instance, incorporating DDL, with its focus on fresh groceries, as part of a larger e-commerce platform could result in greater efficiency and cost savings, which could improve margins and profitability by extension.

A larger company like, for instance, JD.com (JD) could, on paper, use a smaller company like DDL. JD, or more exactly JD Fresh, is already in the grocery business, but it is lagging behind in the segment versus other players. So adding DDL makes sense, as it would fit in with what JD is already doing. JD has also been rather active with acquisitions in order to grow the platform.

This includes, for instance, the acquisition of Dada Nexus in 2025, which offers on-demand delivery, something that would match up well with DDL, at least on paper. As shown previously, DDL trades at relatively low valuations, even after the recent increase in share prices. If an offer is made, it does not have to come at too high a cost for JD. JD is also big enough with enough financial resources to facilitate an offer.

On the other hand, there are no guarantees DDL will fetch interest from suitors. While DDL is available at relatively low valuations and it is true DDL has greatly improved the bottom line in recent years to the point it is now profitable, there are aspects to DDL that may cause potential suitors to walk away after taking a look at DDL.

For instance, while DDL has made great strides with the bottom line, the same cannot be said of the top line. Revenue in recent years has gone flat, with DDL only managing a CAGR of less than one percent in the last three years. The problem with this is that the grocery business is a low-margin business, which means large volumes are needed to generate decent returns. This requires sales growth.

The lack of meaningful sales growth raises the possibility DDL may have already reached its peak in terms of growth potential. The flip side of this is that it could help facilitate a deal for DDL because if DDL feels it has gone as far as it can, at least as an independent entity, DDL is more likely to agree to any proposed acquisition.

Future improvements to the bottom line may be harder to come by for DDL, especially with the lack of growth in the top line, which suggests it may be better to seal a deal while DDL can still boast of the major gains in the bottom line of the past few years. If some other company feels it can get growth started again by incorporating DDL into a larger network that has the reach and scale to service a wider audience, then these dynamics could result in parties coming together.

It is for prospective buyers to decide how far they are willing to go to acquire a company like DDL with its strong and weak points, but DDL should fetch a premium, although opinions may vary as to how much the company should get. Something like 25-35% of the current enterprise value is fair, in my opinion, after taking into account the pros and cons of DDL. This includes the improvement in earnings, but also the lack of sales growth. If enterprise value is currently $415M, then that suggests something in the $519-$560M range.

This may not seem like much since it is below what the company went for as recently as early 2025, but keep in mind that prospective buyers are under no pressure to make a deal. There are many competitors in the grocery business, and new ones may come at any time. The grocery business in China is one where someone has to move a lot of merchandise for not a whole lot of profits. A company like JD may come to decide that it is better to go on their own and build out their own grocery business, including by capturing whatever market share is held by DDL.

In contrast, DDL may be the one that has to make a deal sooner rather than later. While DDL has improved the bottom line, further improvements in the long run will require an expanding top line, and that is currently missing. The lack of significant sales growth will eventually show up in the numbers long term, so it is probably better for DDL to strike a deal before that happens.

Investor Takeaways

DDL only went public in 2021, but in the five years since then, the company has made major strides, especially when it comes to the bottom line. In the years since DDL went public, DDL improved many facets of the business, including margins, free cash flow, operating profit, net profit, and so on. As a result, DDL can boast of having an income statement and a balance sheet that are both in solid shape.

However, DDL has not fared so well in every aspect. For instance, revenue growth has been rather flat in recent years. This lack of meaningful growth is a reflection of the state of the market for groceries, which is characterized by fierce competition. Not only does DDL face a number of competitors, but these competitors tend to be much bigger than DDL with far more resources to deploy. BABA, PDD, and Meituan in particular seem to be doing better.

While DDL has made much progress in recent years, there is reason to ask how DDL will fare over the long run competing in a tough market for groceries against much bigger competitors with much deeper pockets. DDL has been able to get out of the red, but with a net profit margin of 1-2%, it would not take much to get back into the red.

DDL could be better off if it were to join forces with a bigger entity that has the resources to scale up a business like groceries, where scale is everything. In fact, it is probably better for DDL if a sale is agreed upon sooner rather than later because it can still point to all the progress made in recent years as leverage to fetch a better price.

While DDL has made great strides in improving the bottom line, the lack of growth in the top line will eventually make it difficult to keep up the improvement in the bottom line. The longer DDL waits, the more likely it becomes that the rate of improvement starts to stall and the less attractive DDL becomes as an acquisition at a premium.

DDL may not be for everyone, but there are companies out there that could use having DDL in the fold. JD is probably the most likely candidate if a proposal were to surface because DDL would fit in nicely, and it helps that JD has been rather active when it comes to acquisitions. DDL is also within reach with an enterprise value of just $415M. Even if a premium of 25-35% is included to seal a deal, it would not break the bank. A big company like JD or someone similar can afford it.

However, while a proposal to acquire DDL is possible, and arguably needed in DDL’s own interest, there are no guarantees one is forthcoming. If there are no proposals and DDL stays on as an independent and smaller company, it is not impossible for DDL to lose market share to bigger competitors. DDL may begin to shrink as a company in what is not an easy market to succeed in the long run. If this happens and DDL starts to post losses, DDL will no longer fetch as high a price as it does now, and it could come to regret not pulling the trigger when it was in a better position.

I am thus neutral on DDL with everything above in mind. DDL’s future looks kind of iffy if it continues as a standalone company with the market for groceries as is. While DDL has made substantial progress, progress is likely to become harder to come by in the future. Unless it can find a way to boost sales growth, growth is more likely to become constrained for several reasons, including the nature of the grocery business itself and competition against bigger companies. DDL’s chances of growing over the long term are better if it joins forces with someone else, but there are no guarantees this will happen.

data.title: Great Lakes Dredge & Dock Corporation: Solid Setup With Visible Earnings Growth
data.description:
Investment Action

I give a buy rating for Great Lakes Dredge & Dock Corporation (GLDD) as I like the setup. GLDD has strong forward revenue visibility, a backlog skewed toward higher-margin work, and a cost structure that is improving as new vessels enter the fleet. At the same time, offshore wind is a new earnings driver that comes with contracted demand.

Company Description

GLDD is one of the largest dredging contractors in the United States, operating a large fleet of hopper, hydraulic cutter suction, and mechanical dredges. To put it simply, this is a niche marine infrastructure business. GLDD works mainly as a contractor for the US Army Corps of Engineers, with some exposure to private port operators and energy-related clients. There are four main scopes of work here: (1) capital dredging, which involves deepening ports and channels so they can handle larger vessels and heavier traffic; (2) coastal protection, which focuses on beach replenishment and barrier island construction; (3) maintenance dredging, which is the recurring work; and (4) offshore energy, which is the newest area, which focuses on subsea rock installation that supports offshore wind foundations.

A Solid Backlog

The biggest worry for a business like GLDD is that it is inherently a project-based business, and such businesses tend to have poor revenue visibility, especially when it comes to dealing with government entities. Fortunately for GLDD, this is not the case. As of the end of Q3 2025, GLDD had a dredging backlog of $934.5 million (this basically covers the entire FY24 revenue, by the way). That figure itself is already comforting, but what gives even more comfort is that GLDD has an effective backlog of >$1.1 billion. This effective backlog includes ~$193.5 million of low bids and pending awards that have not yet been formally booked. With $1.1 billion of effective backlog, GLDD can effectively cover the next 15 months of revenue easily. I’d take this level of visibility vs. the typical project-based business anytime, not only because it makes it easier to forecast growth, but also because it means GLLD can better plan fleet utilization (more efficiently). Since GLDD already knows the timelines and schedules, it is able to avoid idle time for its fleet, which means margins should be more stable (i.e., remember costs are fixed, so idle time is simply a drag to margin).

The quality of this backlog is also noteworthy. This is not a backlog that is filled with low-return work that ties up assets for years. ~84% of the current backlog relates to capital dredging and coastal protection. These are very complex projects that require specialized vessels and engineering expertise, and that means GLDD should be able to take on better pricing here. This is unlike maintenance dredging, which is really more standardized work. Higher pricing should translate into higher margins (management confirmed in the Q3 2025 call that these works yield higher margins), and with ~84% of them leaning towards these higher-margin works, I expect GLDD margin to trend up as these backlogs translate into revenue.

New Fleet

GLDD is currently undergoing its most aggressive fleet renewal program ever. In August 2025, GLDD took delivery of the Amelia Island (a new hopper dredge) that went straight into active service while older vessels like the Terrapin Island are being retired. I like the timing of this renewal, as GLDD is about to deal with very complex work in the backlog. Doing that with aging ships is expensive since they require more maintenance, burn more fuel, and spend more time in dry dock. By replacing them with newer, more automated vessels, GLDD should be able to more efficiently work through projects in the backlog, pulling forward the timeline of revenue recognition, and also bring down breakeven cost. We already saw early evidence of the latter in Q3 2025. Gross margin expanded to 22.4%, up >300 bps y/y, mainly due to a cleaner cost base from the newer equipment fleet. When GLDD starts to deploy new vessels like the Galveston Island, we should see more of such margin expansion as the overall fleet itself becomes more efficient.

Offshore Wind Catalyst

One important catalyst for the GLDD equity story is offshore wind. This is a new growth vector that GLDD just got into via the Acadia (a US-flagged subsea rock installation vessel) that is scheduled for delivery in Q1 2026. For those unaware, this vessel is designed specifically for offshore wind foundation work, which must be performed using Jones Act-compliant ships. Because of this regulatory requirement, it creates a very small supply pool that can work on these projects. For GLDD, investors are not carrying much risk here since the Acadia is already fully contracted for 2026 on the Empire Wind and Sunrise Wind projects. Which means, as soon as the vessel gets into service, it should start to contribute revenue.

This should be a net positive for GLDD’s equity story, as offshore wind work typically carries higher margins than traditional dredging, and more importantly, it reduces GLDD’s dependence on government budgets. I said “net positive” because I get that sentiment around offshore wind has not been great so far given the cost inflation, project delays, and also Trump’s hatred towards this sector. But I don’t think that is the right framing because the specific service GLDD provides (subsea rock installation for foundations) is a required step regardless of who manufactures the turbine or which developer finances the project. The reality is offshore projects would need scour protection (this is a mandatory engineering step). In offshore wind, the turbines are not the only things to be installed. The high-voltage cables connecting these farms to the grid also require rock mattresses for protection. As such, I would think the demand is much more durable than expected, as GLDD is effectively providing the plumbing and foundation work for offshore energy.

Valuation

Consensus currently models revenue growth to be ~11% in FY25, ~5% in FY26, followed by ~10% in FY27. I agree with the direction, but I don’t see a reason why FY26 revenue growth should decelerate given the visible backlogs and offshore wind catalyst. I would expect GLDD to continue ~10% growth for the next two years, pushing revenue closer to ~$1 billion. Gross margin should expand instead of contract. Assuming gross margin moves up to ~25% by FY27 (due to newer fleets and higher-margin projects), GLDD should deliver ~$250 million in gross profits. Operating costs shouldn’t be moving in line with gross profits given the fixed cost leverage. Say that fixed cost grows to ~$100 million (from ~$80 million in FY24), GLDD should do EBIT of $150 million in FY27; pre-tax profit of ~$125 million ($25 million of interest expense based on ~$415 million debt at 6% rate); and net profit of ~$100 million. Attaching GLDD’s past two-year average NTM PE here of 12x equates to a market cap of $1.2 billion, or a share price of ~$17.6.

Risk

The key structural risk is project execution. Given that these are fixed-price contracts, if there are unexpected costs (due to seabed conditions or severe weather), contract margins could be heavily compressed. As much as GLDD is diversifying into the offshore energy space, it is still heavily reliant on annual government budgets. Any political gridlock or reduction in funding could delay project awards.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating on GLDD. The large backlog, an improving cost base from fleet renewal, and the offshore wind catalyst create a favorable setup. Margins should trend higher as complex projects move from backlog to P&L and newer vessels reduce operating costs. Offshore wind helps GLDD in diversification and provides visible incremental earnings given the contracted nature of the work. W

data.title: Boston Pizza: Still A Strong Royalty Generator
data.description:
I definitely don’t regret upgrading Boston Pizza (BPZZF) and putting it in the same bucket as my other Canadian Royalty Fund picks, alongside Diversified (DIV:CA) and Pizza Pizza (PZA:CA), both last year and again this year.

The market’s view on this type of investment hasn’t really changed over the past couple of years.

You get low exposure to the operational headaches of a traditional restaurant model, relatively predictable cash flows that often behave like a partial inflation hedge, and a hefty yield.

In other words, pretty much everything an income-focused investor hopes for, especially when geopolitical noise is rising and the S&P is beginning to feel expensive.

If you’ve been following my work, you’ll remember that I used to be more skeptical about Boston Pizza’s valuation relative to its Royalty Fund peers.

I changed that view in the last article for three reasons:

  1. Same-store sales ran in the mid-single digits throughout FY 2025 after the promo mix was adjusted around a fixed-price $15 Deal.
  2. Higher same-store sales fed into distributable cash, and distributions followed, growing at roughly an 8% CAGR over the past three years.
  3. With distributions moving higher, the payout is still sitting at comfortable levels (around 88.8% in Q3 FY 2025), which gives some confidence that distributions can keep climbing without the fund having to lean on debt.

Even looking back and noticing that I could have changed the rating earlier, my last slap on 'Buy' here delivered a total return of ~13.2%, far exceeding the S&P which delivered 7.3% in the same stretch.

Not bad, if you ask me.

Among the three names I mentioned earlier, Boston Pizza only trailed Diversified, which isn't even a pure restaurant fund.

That says a lot.

Even in what was a fairly soft quarter for QSR pizza in Canada, Boston Pizza held up well, in large part because it operates as a full-service concept and had already adjusted its fixed-price promotions ahead of the curve.

But what about the road ahead? Does the thesis still hold up?

Q3 FY 2025 gives us a pretty good place to start.

Royalties Growing in a Sustainable Way

As I've mentioned in a few recent write-ups, Canada isn't exactly in a great spot on the labor front.

Unemployment is still elevated at around 6.8%, consumer confidence remains soft, and food inflation reaccelerated in December to 6.2%.

Even so, wages keep growing faster than inflation, which itself is fairly contained at about 2.4%. In that backdrop, Canadians—especially in Ontario—are still spending surprisingly well on discretionary categories.

To see this, I always like to take a look at RBC's Consumer Tracker.

If you line this up with Boston Pizza’s Q3 FY 2025, credit card spending in Canada was up about 0.8% from August to September and then accelerated to roughly 1.2% into year-end. Dining, however, was clearly softer, growing just 0.4% in Q3 and flattening out in Q4.

You get a cleaner read on the consumer when you line up the year-over-year numbers. September, for instance, was running at roughly 7–8% real growth once you strip out inflation.

In Boston Pizza's case, the chain kept its streak alive with a fourth straight quarter of positive same-store sales, likely catching a bit of the 'Fast-Food Exodus'. We’ve seen this movie before in the U.S. with Chili’s (EAT) and Applebee’s (DIN), where casual dining suddenly reopened a significant gap in traffic and perceived value against fast-food.

Here’s how Boston Pizza stacked up on same-store sales versus other Canadian restaurant chains:

Q3 FY 2025 was another good print, with same-store sales up 4.1%. It cooled a bit from the 6.4% pace of the previous quarter, but year-to-date comps are still sitting around 5%, which is nothing to complain about.

That’s obviously good news for unitholders.

At the end of the day, the fund can structurally grow distributable cash in two ways:

  1. Higher same-store sales, because royalties are collected straight off gross restaurant sales; and
  2. Addition of new restaurants to the royalty pool.

And with the footprint basically flat year-over-year at 372 restaurants, the entire 4.3% lift in system sales to $248.9 million came straight from same-store sales growth.

That’s exactly why I said back in February that same-store sales matter more than ever when it comes to driving distribution growth.

You can see it in the cash numbers too.

Distributable cash rose 4.9% year-over-year to $8.5 million, or $0.40 per unit, leaving the payout ratio at a very reasonable 88.8% (in Q2 FY 2025, for example, the payout was at 92.3%).

At this point, you might be wondering why distributable cash is growing faster than system-wide sales. Fair question. But the answer is simpler than it seems: operational micro-leverage from double-digit reductions in SG&A.

In my view, even if same-store sales cool off a bit going forward (most likely in Q1 FY 2026 thanks to snowstorms) they should still be able to maintain the payout at comfortable levels at ~90%.

Some Risks Along the Way

On top of the risk that same-store sales cool off and the payout window tightens a bit, I also want to stress-test this thesis under a few alternative scenarios.

To be fair, most of them don't worry me all that much, but a few do put a cap on how fast this fund can grow.

The first and most obvious one is the stalled pipeline of new store openings.

That leaves the fund increasingly dependent on same-store sales to drive growth, as I mentioned earlier. And this is a bit of an unusual year. Consumers have been leaning more toward full-service dining, comparisons are easy, and promotional activity is heavy.

Mid-single-digit same-store growth is still possible, but it's clearly a tougher task from here. Over time, these royalty funds usually end up delivering something closer to structural unit growth plus inflation, with maybe a small mix growth on top.

That’s why I prefer to keep my assumptions grounded and lean on long-term inflation or GDP growth when modeling dividend growth over the long haul.

The second risk is macro.

Consumption is still holding up, but I’d expect a bit of cooling in the first half of the year as the labor market stays soft and households feel more pressure from essential spending. That naturally squeezes the discretionary dollars flowing into restaurants.

If things tighten up, lower-priced limited-service chains like McDonald’s (MCD)—with promotions like the $5 McValue in Canada—can easily siphon some traffic away from full-service names like Boston Pizza.

It is the relative value being replaced by the absolute value of price points.

Does Valuation Still Make Sense?

As I've laid out before, if you take the $1.44 per unit in dividends, grow it in perpetuity roughly in line with long-term inflation at 2%, and discount it at about 7%, you end up with a fair value in the ballpark of $28 to $30 per share.

With the risk-free rate (10-year Canada bonds) sitting around 3.4%, the TSX averaging roughly 8% annually, and a beta of 0.78, a ~7% cost of equity still looks reasonable to me.

That said, I'd rather roll the model forward using the FY 2026 dividend of $1.55 per unit and stress a few scenarios with slightly slower dividend growth.

At a 1.5% growth rate, fair value comes out closer to $28, and at 1% it’s around $26. Both still comfortably above the roughly $23.40 where Boston Pizza is trading today.

Even in a world where dividends stay flat forever, the stock would only fall to roughly $22, which is about 5% down.

Looking at it another way, Pizza Pizza trades at a P/E of 16.9. Plugging in the consensus EPS of $1.82 for FY 2026 gives a fair value of around $30, which fits neatly with the 2% long-term growth in the Gordon Model. Using Pizza Pizza’s 16.7x P/CF and $1.84 in OCF per share gives us the same fair value.

I’d go with a target near $28, around 20% upside, and a 6.6% yield. All told, that adds up to more than 25% in annual return.

Pretty solid, if you ask me.

So I’ll once again stick with my 'Buy' rating, assuming they keep outperforming the S&P by delivering steady, low-risk returns.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
